title: Auditing 64 Articles for Missing Hero Images — What I Found
content:
![Auditing 64 Articles for Missing Hero Images — What I Found](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

ShabelleHub's blog has 64 articles in Supabase — 23 published, 41 in draft. At a glance, scrolling through the published list, something looked inconsistent: some posts opened with a polished dark-gradient hero graphic, title text laid over it, a small category badge in the corner. Others opened straight into a paragraph of text, no image at all. The question was simple — how many, and which ones?

Guessing wasn't good enough. This called for an actual audit against the database, not a scroll-and-eyeball check.

## Querying for the Real Numbers

The hero images on this site aren't uploaded files — they're inline SVGs, base64-encoded, embedded directly in the markdown content as the first line of the post: `![Title](data:image/svg+xml;base64,...)`. That makes them queryable as a text pattern:

```sql
SELECT slug, status,
  (doc->>'content' LIKE '![%](data:image/svg+xml%') as has_svg_hero,
  (doc->>'featured_image_url' IS NOT NULL) as has_featured_url
FROM posts;
```

The result was a clean split. All 41 draft articles — the "Building in Public" series — had the inline SVG hero pattern. Of the 23 published articles, exactly 10 had it too: specifically, the ones that started as "Building in Public" drafts and had already been promoted to published. The remaining 23 published articles had neither an inline SVG nor a `featured_image_url`. Zero hero image, full stop.

That's not a sizing inconsistency, which is what it first looked like from a distance. It's a coverage gap — an entire batch of the site's original content (comparison guides, prompt-engineering guides, "best AI tools for X" roundups) was published before the hero-image pattern existed, and nobody had gone back to backfill it.

## Building the Missing 23, Matching the Existing Style

Rather than invent a new visual style, the fix was to replicate the existing SVG template exactly — same 1200×630 canvas, same dark gradient background, same diagonal line texture, same glowing circle motif in the corner, same category badge, same "ShabelleHub" wordmark bottom-left. Only two things change per article: the badge text (pulled from the post's actual category — "Guide" or "Comparison") and the title, word-wrapped to fit a maximum of three lines at the same font size used everywhere else.

A small script handled the generation: build the SVG as a string, wrap the title with Python's `textwrap`, base64-encode the result, and produce a `jsonb_set` SQL statement that prepends the image markdown to each post's existing `content` field — the same pattern already used for the 41 drafts.

## Verifying Before Touching Production

Here's the part that mattered most: none of this ran against the live database until it was checked. Every generated SVG was validated as well-formed XML. Every one was confirmed to declare the correct 1200×630 dimensions. Every title's word-wrap was checked against a 3-line, ~26-character-per-line limit — the same constraint the original hand-built images respected — to make sure nothing overflowed or got cut off.

That validation caught its own false alarm along the way: an early check flagged several titles as wrapping to "4 lines," which looked like a real problem until closer inspection showed the script was also counting the "Hub" half of the "ShabelleHub" logo text at the bottom of the image as a fourth title line. Once the check was scoped correctly — counting only the tspans inside the actual title block — all 23 came back within the same 2–3 line limit as the rest of the site.

23 statements, 23 unique slugs, zero duplicates, zero XML errors, zero dimension mismatches. Only after all of that passed did the SQL file get handed over — as a file to review and run deliberately in the Supabase SQL editor, not as something auto-applied.

## Why the Order of Operations Matters

It would have been faster to generate the SVGs and run the SQL in the same step. It also would have meant the first person to notice any problem — a title that wrapped wrong, a badge that didn't fit, a malformed base64 string — would have been a site visitor looking at a broken hero image on a published article, not a validation script catching it in a sandbox first.

Separating "generate and verify" from "apply to production" is a small amount of extra process. On a site with 64 live articles and no staging environment in the traditional sense, it's also the difference between a mistake that costs a few minutes to regenerate and a mistake that's visible to every reader until someone notices and fixes it. Every one of the fixes made to this site during this stretch of work followed the same shape: generate or patch first, verify against the actual data or the actual rendered structure second, and only then apply it to what visitors see. It's slower per change. It's also the reason none of those changes needed to be rolled back.

**Takeaway:** an inconsistency that looks like a styling problem is sometimes a coverage gap instead — and the fix for a coverage gap should get the same verification discipline as a fix for a bug, even though nothing was technically broken.
excerpt: ShabelleHub's blog has 64 articles in Supabase — 23 published, 41 in draft. At a glance, scrolling through the published list, something looked inconsistent: some posts opened with a polished dark-gradient hero graphic,…
author: Mohamed Abdi Guled
seoTitle: Auditing 64 Articles for Missing Hero Images — What I Found
seoDescription: ShabelleHub's blog has 64 articles in Supabase — 23 published, 41 in draft. At a glance, scrolling through the published list, something looked inconsistent: some posts opened with a polished dark-gradient hero graphic,…

title: Diagnosing an ISP Block: When Your Own Site Times Out on Your Own Phone
content:
![Diagnosing an ISP Block: When Your Own Site Times Out on Your Own Phone](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The deployment succeeded. Vercel showed the build green, no errors in the logs, the previous version of the site had been loading fine minutes earlier. Then the URL just... didn't load. Not an error page, not a 500, not a broken layout — the browser sat there until Chrome gave up with `ERR_CONNECTION_TIMED_OUT`. The kind of failure that looks exactly like the server is down, except the server wasn't down.

## The First Wrong Assumption

The obvious first suspects were the obvious first suspects: had the latest deploy broken something, was Vercel having an outage, had a DNS record gotten misconfigured. None of those held up. Vercel's dashboard showed the deployment as healthy. The build logs showed no errors. And — the detail that eventually mattered — a different device on a different network loaded the site instantly, no delay, no timeout, nothing.

Same site. Same URL. Same moment in time. One connection times out, another loads clean. That rules out the server, the deployment, and the DNS record all at once — if any of those were actually broken, every connection would fail the same way, not just some of them.

## Isolating the Variable

With the server ruled out, the only thing left that differed between "works" and "doesn't work" was the network path itself. A quick test made that concrete: switch on a VPN, reload the same URL on the same phone. It loaded immediately.

That's a specific, informative result. A VPN doesn't touch the website, the DNS record, or the hosting provider — it changes the network route the request takes to get there. If turning on a VPN is the only variable that flips a timeout into a successful load, the problem lives somewhere in that network path, not on the server end at all.

## Naming the Actual Cause

The pattern matches a known behavior: some ISPs block or throttle access to shared hosting subdomains — `.vercel.app`, in this case — sometimes because of aggressive spam or phishing block-lists that were built for a different site entirely and happen to catch every project hosted on the same shared domain. The site's own custom domain, if it had one, generally wouldn't be affected the same way; the block was targeting the shared subdomain pattern, not the specific project.

That explanation fit every piece of evidence collected: consistent failure on one specific carrier's network, consistent success on a VPN or a different SIM, zero errors anywhere in the actual hosting infrastructure, and a failure mode (silent timeout, no error page) that's characteristic of a network-level block rather than an application-level one.

## Checking With the Source, Not Assuming

A theory that fits the evidence still isn't confirmation. The next step was contacting the ISPs directly — both Somnet and Hormuud — through their support channels, describing the exact symptom and asking directly whether `.vercel.app` domains were being blocked or throttled on their network.

The answers were unhelpful in an informative way. Both support channels responded with generic customer-service scripts, and one explicitly said the query needed to go to "the relevant team" — confirming, indirectly, that front-line support at a consumer ISP doesn't have visibility into low-level routing or block-list decisions, and isn't going to be the channel that resolves something like this. That's a useful thing to know before spending more time on it: some problems have a real technical answer, and some problems have an answer that lives inside an organization's infrastructure team where a support chatbot will never reach it.

## What Actually Gets Fixed Here

A VPN or a second SIM card solves the symptom on one device, for one person, right now. It isn't a fix — it's a workaround that has to be repeated by every visitor on the affected network, every time, indefinitely. The durable fix for this specific failure mode is moving off the shared subdomain entirely: a custom domain isn't swept up in the same block-lists that target `.vercel.app` broadly, because it isn't sharing infrastructure-level reputation with every other project hosted under that same subdomain pattern.

Until that migration happens, the honest status of the bug is: diagnosed, root cause identified with reasonable confidence, workaround available, permanent fix scoped but not yet applied. That's a different — and more useful — state than "broken, cause unknown," even though the site still isn't reachable on the affected network yet.

## The Diagnostic Habit That Mattered

The single most useful step in this entire process wasn't a tool or a command — it was testing the same request from two different network paths before concluding anything about the server. A timeout on one device looks identical to a server outage until you can compare it against a second device on a different connection. That comparison is what turned "the site is down, no idea why" into "the site is fine, this specific network is blocking it" in the space of one test.

**Takeaway:** when something that should be simple times out with no error and no explanation, don't start with the server. Start by checking whether the same request succeeds from a different network. If it does, you're not debugging your code — you're debugging a network path, and that's a completely different investigation.
excerpt: The deployment succeeded. Vercel showed the build green, no errors in the logs, the previous version of the site had been loading fine minutes earlier. Then the URL just... didn't load. Not an error page, not a 500, not…
author: Mohamed Abdi Guled
seoTitle: Diagnosing an ISP Block: When Your Own Site Times Out on Your Own Phone
seoDescription: The deployment succeeded. Vercel showed the build green, no errors in the logs, the previous version of the site had been loading fine minutes earlier. Then the URL just... didn't load. Not an error page, not a 500, not…

title: Three Mobile Bugs, One Afternoon: Fixing What 64 Live Articles Had in Common
content:
![Three Mobile Bugs, One Afternoon: Fixing What 64 Live Articles Had in Common](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

I run ShabelleHub from a Samsung phone — Termux, GitHub's mobile editor, and a lot of screenshots. Most days that means fixing one thing at a time. This particular afternoon, three separate mobile bugs turned out to share the same root cause: text sized for a desktop screen, shipped without checking what happens when the screen gets smaller.

None of these bugs would show up in a code review. They only show up when you actually hold the phone.

## Bug 1: The 11px Byline

The blog post breadcrumb and byline — "Home › Blog › Building in Public," "By Mohamed Abdi Guled" — were set at 11px, 12px, and 13px across different elements. On a desktop monitor that's a minor stylistic choice. On a 6-inch phone screen at arm's length, it's close to unreadable, and it fails basic accessibility guidance, which treats anything under 12–14px as too small for body text on mobile.

The fix was mechanical once we found every instance:

```javascript
content = content.replace(/fontSize: 11[,}]/g, 'fontSize: 14,');
content = content.replace(/fontSize: 12[,}]/g, 'fontSize: 15,');
content = content.replace(/fontSize: 13[,}]/g, 'fontSize: 14,');
```

Not elegant, but precise — a targeted anchor-based patch, not a redesign. The header `clamp()` value also got a wider floor, so the title no longer risked shrinking toward unreadable on narrow viewports.

## Bug 2: A 42px Tap Target

The mobile hamburger menu button measured 42×42px. The generally accepted minimum comfortable tap target on mobile is 44×44px — small enough that most people never notice it's off, large enough that thumbs miss it more than they should. Two pixels doesn't sound like much until you're the person who fat-fingered the wrong nav item on a bus.

Fix: 42px → 44px. One line, verified against the accessibility guideline it maps to, shipped.

## Bug 3: Code Blocks That Ran Off the Screen

This one only showed up in a screenshot someone sent me — a fenced code block (`// next.config.js`) with its right edge simply gone, no visible scrollbar, no obvious way to see the rest of the line. On mobile Chrome, `overflow-x: auto` alone doesn't always give you a clear, discoverable scroll gesture, particularly when the container also lacks an explicit `max-width`.

The fix added two things to the existing rule:

```css
overflow-x: auto;
-webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch;
max-width: 100%;
```

`max-width: 100%` keeps the block from claiming more horizontal space than its container allows. `-webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch` gives iOS and Android WebKit a proper momentum-scroll gesture inside the block instead of a stiff, easy-to-miss drag.

This function is shared across every blog post template — markdown-rendered and block-rendered alike — so the one patch applied to every article on the site at once, not just the one where the bug was spotted.

## How We Found Them

None of these three bugs came from a planned audit. Two came from screenshots — a reader (in this case, the site owner) forwarding a photo of the phone screen with a question attached: "why is this cut off?" The third came from applying general accessibility guidance to a page that hadn't been checked against it recently.

That's worth saying plainly: automated tools like Lighthouse will flag some of this — contrast ratios, some tap-target sizing — but they won't catch everything. Lighthouse doesn't know that a code block's right edge disappears off-screen if the container technically has `overflow-x: auto` set; it can pass an audit and still fail a real reader holding a real phone. The screenshot-driven bug report, unglamorous as it is, still catches things automation misses.

## Why These Three, Together

Individually, none of these is dramatic. A byline two points too small. A button two pixels too tight. A code block with a hidden edge. But they share a pattern worth naming: each one was invisible on the machine that built it and obvious on the device most readers actually use.

Individually, none of these is dramatic. A byline two points too small. A button two pixels too tight. A code block with a hidden edge. But they share a pattern worth naming: each one was invisible on the machine that built it and obvious on the device most readers actually use.

Somewhere around 60% of traffic to a site like this arrives on a phone. Testing exclusively on a laptop means testing against the minority experience. The fix isn't complicated — actually load the page on a phone before calling something done — but it's the step that's easiest to skip when you're moving fast.

## What We Didn't Touch

Font sizes, tap targets, and overflow behavior are functional accessibility issues — they affect whether people can read and interact with the content. We treated them differently from layout and visual design, which stayed untouched throughout: no color changes, no spacing overhaul, no component redesign. The distinction matters. Not every mobile complaint is a design problem; some of them are just bugs that happen to only be visible on a phone.

## The Boring Part That Made This Safe

Every fix here followed the same discipline: find the exact string in the file, confirm it's unique, patch only that string, run the linter, review the diff line by line, and only then commit. That process is slower than eyeballing a component and guessing at a fix. It's also the reason three separate CSS-adjacent changes went out the same afternoon without breaking anything else on 64 live articles.

The diff for the code-block fix, for example, was exactly two properties added to one existing style string — nothing reformatted, nothing restructured, no unrelated whitespace changes riding along. When a change is that narrow, reviewing it takes seconds and the risk of a silent regression drops close to zero. That's a deliberate trade: less clever, more verifiable.

**Takeaway:** if you're testing a site primarily on desktop, you're testing the smaller share of your audience. Pull up the mobile view before you ship — not after a reader sends you a screenshot.
excerpt: I run ShabelleHub from a Samsung phone — Termux, GitHub's mobile editor, and a lot of screenshots. Most days that means fixing one thing at a time. This particular afternoon, three separate mobile bugs turned out to shar…
author: Mohamed Abdi Guled
seoTitle: Three Mobile Bugs, One Afternoon: Fixing What 64 Live Articles Had in Common
seoDescription: I run ShabelleHub from a Samsung phone — Termux, GitHub's mobile editor, and a lot of screenshots. Most days that means fixing one thing at a time. This particular afternoon, three separate mobile bugs turned out to shar…

title: Chasing a PageSpeed Score That Kept Changing While the Code Stayed the Same
content:
![Chasing a PageSpeed Score That Kept Changing While the Code Stayed the Same](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Same URL. Same code. Same deployment. Four PageSpeed Insights runs across roughly twenty-five minutes returned scores of 68, 90, 91, and 77. Nothing was deployed between any of those runs. Nothing in the codebase changed. If performance testing is supposed to measure the performance of a specific version of a site, four different answers for one unchanged version is either a serious hidden problem or a measurement problem — and those two possibilities call for very different responses.

## The Instinct to Chase the Number

The first run — 68, with a reported Largest Contentful Paint of 5.0 seconds — looked like a real regression worth investigating immediately. The natural next move is to start looking for what broke: check recent commits, inspect the hero component for a rendering issue, look for a runtime error that only shows up under certain conditions.

Before going down that path, the same URL got tested again. 90. Then again, a few minutes later: 91. The instinct to immediately start debugging the code was, in hindsight, premature — nothing had changed between the 68 and the 90 except the clock.

## Ruling Out an Actual Regression

Jumping to "the code is fine, it's just noise" without checking would have been just as much of a guess as jumping to "the code is broken." Both needed verification, not assumption.

Vercel's deployment history and runtime error logs were checked directly: zero runtime errors across the relevant window, and the only recent deployment had finished well before the first low-scoring run started. That ruled out the most obvious explanation — a fresh deploy still warming up its cache, known as a cold start, where the very first requests after a deployment are measurably slower than requests made once the page has been served a few times and cached.

The specific components most likely to cause a real, code-level LCP problem were reviewed directly: the animated hero background and a component rendering the top few tools. Neither used client-side data fetching gated behind a loading state, and neither depended on JavaScript executing before content appeared — both rendered their content as part of the initial server-rendered HTML. If either had been silently waiting on a `useEffect` or a client fetch before showing real content, that would have been a legitimate, reproducible cause of a slow LCP. Neither was.

## Reading the Report Against Itself

The specific report showing the 68 score had its own internal contradiction, once inspected closely: it broke the Largest Contentful Paint timing down into components — time to first byte and render delay — and those two numbers summed to roughly 680 milliseconds. The same report's headline LCP figure was 3.6 seconds. A report whose own sub-measurements don't add up to its own headline number is a strong, specific signal that the measurement itself is unstable, not that the page is unstable.

That detail mattered more than any of the score comparisons. It wasn't "the numbers seem to bounce around" — it was "this specific report's own math doesn't add up," which is a much harder thing to explain by pointing at the website's code.

## What Lab Testing Actually Measures

Lighthouse — the engine behind PageSpeed Insights — runs a single simulated page load under artificial network throttling and CPU slowdown meant to approximate a mid-range mobile device on a slow connection. It's explicitly a single-sample measurement of a simulated environment, not a running average of how real visitors experience the site. Google's own documentation is direct about this: a single Lighthouse run can vary meaningfully from the next run of the exact same page, because the simulated environment itself has variance built into it — which is exactly why Google also provides an entirely separate reporting layer, the Chrome User Experience Report, built from real visitor data aggregated over a rolling 28-day window specifically because single lab runs aren't reliable enough to trust in isolation.

Four runs of the same unchanged page landing at 68, 90, 91, and 77 isn't inconsistent with that documented behavior. It's close to the expected shape of it.

## Where the Investigation Actually Stopped

The other scores in every one of those four reports told a more consistent story than the headline performance number did: Cumulative Layout Shift measured zero in every run, Total Blocking Time stayed low across all of them, and Accessibility, Best Practices, and SEO scores didn't move at all between runs. If something in the code had actually regressed, it's unlikely every other independent metric would have stayed rock steady while only the LCP-driven performance score swung by more than twenty points.

The investigation stopped there — not because every question had a perfectly satisfying answer, but because every piece of available evidence pointed the same direction, and continuing to chase a single lab number past that point would have meant optimizing for noise instead of for anything a real visitor experiences.

**Takeaway:** before treating a performance score drop as a regression, run the test again. If the number moves significantly with zero code changes in between, you're not debugging the website — you're debugging the measurement, and the fix for that is patience and real-user data, not another round of changes to code that was never actually the problem.
excerpt: Same URL. Same code. Same deployment. Four PageSpeed Insights runs across roughly twenty-five minutes returned scores of 68, 90, 91, and 77. Nothing was deployed between any of those runs. Nothing in the codebase changed…
author: Mohamed Abdi Guled
seoTitle: Chasing a PageSpeed Score That Kept Changing While the Code Stayed the Same
seoDescription: Same URL. Same code. Same deployment. Four PageSpeed Insights runs across roughly twenty-five minutes returned scores of 68, 90, 91, and 77. Nothing was deployed between any of those runs. Nothing in the codebase changed…

title: The Bug That Wasn't a Bug: Chasing a Phantom Content Issue for an Hour
content:
![The Bug That Wasn't a Bug: Chasing a Phantom Content Issue for an Hour](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

A screenshot arrived showing a blog post with a hero image, a title, a short excerpt callout — and then nothing. No body text. Just white space where 17,000 characters of article content should have been. The obvious read: the content was missing, corrupted, or never saved. We'd seen exactly that failure mode before on this site, on a different post, a few days earlier. So that's where the investigation started.

It's worth walking through the hour that followed, because the actual bug turned out to be somewhere else entirely — and the process of ruling things out is often more instructive than the fix itself.

## Step One: Check If the Data Exists

Before touching any code, the first move was querying Supabase directly:

```sql
SELECT slug, length(doc->>'content') as content_length
FROM posts WHERE slug = 'adsense-csp-headers-nextjs';
```

Result: 17,268 characters. The content was there — not empty, not null, not truncated. That ruled out the most likely explanation in about ten seconds.

## Step Two: Check the Rendering Logic

If the data exists but doesn't render, the next suspect is the template. The page component has a branch: render structured `content_blocks` if they exist, otherwise fall back to rendering the raw `content` field as markdown. That fallback function had a null check — `if (!md) return ''` — which looked, at first glance, like exactly the kind of thing that could silently swallow content if a field name didn't match between what Supabase returns and what the component expects.

That's a real category of bug, and it's happened on this codebase before: a Firestore-style document gets flattened into a Supabase JSONB `doc` column, and if the code that unpacks it doesn't spread the fields correctly, `post.content` comes back `undefined` even though the data is sitting right there in the database.

So we traced the exact function responsible for building the `post` object — `toPost()` — line by line. It spreads the document fields directly onto the post object with `...d`. Correct. The data would flow through cleanly. Rendering logic: also not the bug.

## Step Three: Actually Scroll Down

At this point the investigation had ruled out the two most likely causes and found nothing. The next step wasn't a code change — it was going back to the actual page and scrolling past the hero image and the excerpt box, all the way down.

The article was there. All 17,268 characters of it, rendering exactly as expected below the fold. The screenshot that started the investigation simply hadn't captured that far down the page — it showed the hero image, the intro paragraphs, and stopped, giving the visual impression of a page that ended prematurely when it hadn't.

## So What Was Actually Wrong?

Nothing, in that specific case. But the investigation wasn't wasted, because it surfaced a real, separate bug along the way: a code block inside that very article was overflowing horizontally off the edge of the screen on mobile, with no visible affordance to scroll and see the rest of the line. That's a genuine, fixable UX problem — just not the one the original screenshot appeared to show.

## The Lesson in the False Alarm

It's tempting to treat a debugging session that ends in "actually, nothing was wrong" as time lost. It usually isn't. Ruling out the database and the rendering logic with actual queries and actual code review — rather than assumption — is exactly what prevents a wrong fix from shipping. If the instinct had been trusted instead of checked, the likely next step would have been rewriting the markdown renderer or patching a `content_blocks` fallback that didn't need to change, on a codebase where 22 other articles depend on that exact same rendering path working correctly.

A rewrite driven by a wrong diagnosis doesn't just waste the time it takes to write it. It risks breaking something that was already working, for the sake of fixing something that was never broken. On a site where one shared rendering function serves every blog post, a bad "fix" doesn't stay contained to the one article someone was looking at — it ships to all of them the next time the page rebuilds.

## What Changed As a Result

The direct outcome wasn't a content fix — it was the mobile code-block overflow fix that came out of scrolling further into the same article to double-check. That fix shipped separately, verified with its own diff review, and happened to apply to every article on the site that uses a fenced code block, not just the one flagged in the original screenshot.

**Takeaway:** when a bug report says "the content is missing," check whether the content is actually missing before assuming it is. A query against the database and a full scroll through the live page cost a few minutes. Rewriting working code based on an assumption costs a lot more — and sometimes introduces the very bug you were trying to fix.
excerpt: A screenshot arrived showing a blog post with a hero image, a title, a short excerpt callout — and then nothing. No body text. Just white space where 17,000 characters of article content should have been. The obvious rea…
author: Mohamed Abdi Guled
seoTitle: The Bug That Wasn't a Bug: Chasing a Phantom Content Issue for an Hour
seoDescription: A screenshot arrived showing a blog post with a hero image, a title, a short excerpt callout — and then nothing. No body text. Just white space where 17,000 characters of article content should have been. The obvious rea…

title: The Substring Bug: When "gem" Matched "management"
content:
![The Substring Bug: When "gem" Matched "management"](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

A reader typed "gem" into ShabelleHub's search bar, expecting Gemini. What came back included tools tagged "Project Management." Nothing about that result set was visibly broken — no error, no crash, no empty state. It just quietly returned the wrong things, which is a harder bug to notice than one that fails loudly.

## What the Search Was Actually Doing

The site's search matches a typed query against a tool's name, its category, and its tags. The tag-matching logic looked reasonable at a glance:

```javascript
const tagMatch = tool.tags.some(tag =>
  tag.toLowerCase().includes(query.toLowerCase())
);
```

`.includes()` checks whether one string appears anywhere inside another — not just at the start of a word, anywhere. Type "gem" and JavaScript will happily confirm that "gem" appears inside "mana**gem**ent." It's not wrong about that. It's just not what a search box is supposed to mean by "gem."

The same logic affected any query that happened to be a substring of an unrelated word: short, common letter sequences are exactly the ones most likely to turn up buried inside something else entirely. A three- or four-letter query is small enough to hide inside dozens of longer words that have nothing to do with what the person was actually looking for.

## Why It Took a While to Notice

The bug didn't fail every search — most queries are specific enough, or long enough, that an accidental substring match inside an unrelated word is rare. "Claude" isn't going to accidentally match anything else. It's specifically short, generic queries — "gem," "AI," "V" — that expose the problem, because short strings are statistically far more likely to appear as a fragment of some longer, unrelated word.

That's also why it's an easy category of bug to ship without noticing: it passes casual testing. Type a few obvious full tool names while checking that search works, get correct results every time, move on. The failure only shows up with the kind of short, ambiguous input a real user types without thinking twice about it.

## The Fix: Match From the Start of a Word

The intent behind tag search was never "does this string appear anywhere in this tag" — it was "does this tag start with what I typed," the same way autocomplete works almost everywhere else. The fix replaces the substring check with a word-boundary check:

```javascript
function wordStartMatch(text, query) {
  const words = text.toLowerCase().split(/\s+/);
  return words.some(word => word.startsWith(query.toLowerCase()));
}
```

Splitting a tag or category into individual words and checking whether any of them *starts with* the query, rather than checking whether the query appears anywhere inside the raw string, changes the matching behavior exactly where it needed to change. "gem" now matches "Gemini" and matches a tag like "Image Generation" — because "Generation" itself starts with "gen" — but does not match "management," because no word in that phrase starts with "gem."

That second part is worth sitting with: the fix isn't "make search stricter" in a way that only returns exact matches. "Image Generation" showing up for a "gem" query is completely correct — "Generation" does start with "gem." The distinction the fix draws isn't between loose and strict matching, it's between matching at a word boundary versus matching anywhere in a string, which is a more specific and more correct rule than either extreme.

## Verifying the Fix Didn't Overcorrect

The natural risk with any search-matching change is fixing one class of false positive while accidentally introducing false negatives elsewhere — making search too narrow instead of too loose. After the patch shipped, single-letter queries were tested deliberately, precisely because short queries are where this bug lived: searching "V" correctly returned tools tagged with words like "Voice," "Video," and "Vector," each confirmed against the actual tag data in Supabase rather than assumed from the UI alone. Searching "M" continued to correctly surface "Management"-tagged tools when someone actually typed something matching that word — the fix didn't remove that behavior, it just stopped an unrelated three-letter query from stumbling into it by accident.

## The General Shape of This Bug

`.includes()` and `.startsWith()` look interchangeable in a quick read of the code — both are built-in string methods, both take a substring argument, both return a boolean. They express fundamentally different rules. `.includes()` answers "does this appear anywhere," which is the right tool for something like a full-text content search where a match in the middle of a sentence is meaningful. `.startsWith()` — or a word-boundary variant of it — answers "does this begin with," which is the right tool for anything meant to behave like autocomplete or tag matching, where a person expects to be typing the beginning of the word they mean.

Neither is universally correct. The bug here wasn't using `.includes()` — it was using `.includes()` in a context that actually needed word-boundary matching, and the mismatch was invisible until a specific, short, common query exposed it.

**Takeaway:** when a search feature returns technically-correct-but-obviously-wrong results, check whether the match is happening at a word boundary or anywhere in the string. Short queries are where that distinction gets tested first — because they're the ones most likely to hide inside something unrelated.
